title: Rabbit breed selection for meat production
subtitle: Comparing New Zealand White, Californian, Rex, and Flemish Giant on growth rate, feed conversion, and temperament.
excerpt: Choosing the right breed is one of the most important decisions in a small-scale meat rabbit enterprise. Growth rate, feed conversion ratio, carcass weight, and ease of handling all vary significantly between breeds.
sourceNotes: British Rabbit Council breed standards. AHDB meat production guidance. USDA Agricultural Research Service rabbit production literature. FAO small-scale rabbit production handbook.

glossaryTerms.definition: The amount of feed (by weight) required to produce one unit of bodyweight gain. A ratio of 3:1 means the animal eats 3 kg of feed to gain 1 kg of body weight. Lower is more efficient.
The proportion of a live animal's bodyweight that becomes usable carcass after slaughter and dressing. Rabbits typically dress out at 50 to 55 percent of live weight.

The target live weight at which a meat rabbit is slaughtered, typically 2.0 to 2.5 kg. Rabbits taken at this weight have tender meat and a good feed conversion ratio. Taking them heavier increases feed cost without a proportional carcass gain.

content.text: The four breeds most commonly used in small-scale UK meat production are New Zealand White, Californian, Rex, and Flemish Giant. Each has a different profile for growth rate,
feed conversion ratio
, carcass quality, and handling character. No breed is right for every setup.
New Zealand White
New Zealand Whites are the benchmark commercial meat breed worldwide. They reach
fryer weight

(2.0 to 2.5 kg) in 8 to 10 weeks. Feed conversion is typically 3:1 or better. The carcass is well-muscled on the loin and hindquarters. Pelts are white and have limited value. Temperament varies by breeder, but the breed is generally calm with regular handling.
Does are good mothers with large litters of 8 to 10 kits. This is the first choice for anyone focused purely on production efficiency.
Californian
Californians are white with dark-pointed ears, nose, feet, and tail. They are slightly smaller than New Zealand Whites, reaching fryer weight at 9 to 11 weeks.
Dress-out percentage
is similar. They are often crossed with New Zealand Whites to combine the growth rate of the NZW with the Californian's noted calm temperament and meat quality.
Californians are a good pick when handling ease matters as much as output.
Rex

Rex rabbits have a thick, velvety coat from a gene change that alters the guard hairs. The pelt has commercial value, which changes the cost calculation. The Rex grows more slowly than NZW or Californian, typically reaching 2.0 kg at 11 to 13 weeks. The carcass is good but smaller.
Rex is a breed for both pelt and meat. It is not ideal for pure meat production. Rex is calm and easy to handle.
Flemish Giant

Flemish Giants are the largest domestic rabbit breed, with adults reaching 6 to 10 kg. The carcass weight at slaughter is high, but so is the feed cost to get there. Feed conversion ratio is poor compared to the smaller breeds because the animal spends a long time growing before reaching slaughter weight.

Flemish Giants are better used as a terminal sire crossed onto a commercial doe. The crossed kittens grow faster and larger than pure Flemish, with better feed conversion than a purebred. As a pure meat breed, they are not efficient.
Summary comparison
Best growth rate: New Zealand White.
Best temperament: Californian.
Best for dual pelt and meat: Rex.
Best as terminal sire cross: Flemish Giant.
Best all-round production efficiency: NZW x Californian cross.
